<!-- ASSUMPTION: The imported RTL register map and top-level module name are provided via environment overrides and/or the generated build collateral. -->
<!-- ASSUMPTION: A Verilator-compatible filelist is available at FILELIST, or can be supplied by the user/CI. -->
<!-- ASSUMPTION: The cocotb testbench and Rust firmware build outputs are available under the firmware workspace generated by the Arch2RTL flow. -->
<!-- ASSUMPTION: The simulation top-level exposes the pwm_controller DUT and observable pwm_out signal consistent with the verified RTL. -->

# PWM Firmware Co-Simulation Runbook

This document describes how to build the imported RTL, build the Rust firmware if needed, and run cocotb-based co-simulation for the PWM fan-control interface.

## Prerequisites

Install and verify the following tools in your environment:

- Verilator
- Python 3
- pip packages:
  - cocotb
  - pytest
  - any cocotb/Verilator integration dependencies used by your environment
- Rust toolchain (if building firmware locally)
  - cargo
  - rustc

Optional but recommended:

- llvm-cov for firmware coverage
- Verilator coverage support for RTL coverage

## Environment Variables

Set or override these as needed:

- `RTL_TOP` : RTL top module name
- `RTL_DIR` : RTL source directory
- `FILELIST` : Verilator filelist for the imported RTL
- `BUILD_DIR` : simulation build directory, default under `firmware/validate/`
- `FW_MANIFEST` : path to the Rust Cargo manifest, if the firmware build is needed
- `COCOTB_TEST_MODULES` : cocotb test module list, if not provided by the testbench defaults

Example overrides:

```bash
export RTL_TOP=pwm_controller
export RTL_DIR=/path/to/imported/rtl
export FILELIST=/path/to/filelist.f
export FW_MANIFEST=/path/to/firmware/Cargo.toml
```

## Build and Run Steps

### 1) Build RTL simulation model with Verilator

Preferred: use the collateral generated in `firmware/validate/verilator_build.md` if present. If that document exists, follow its commands exactly.

Otherwise, run a Verilator build using the imported RTL inputs:

```bash
mkdir -p firmware/validate/build
verilator \
  --cc \
  --exe \
  --build \
  --top-module "${RTL_TOP}" \
  -Mdir firmware/validate/build/verilator \
  --trace \
  --coverage \
  -f "${FILELIST}"
```

If your environment uses a custom cocotb makeflow or wrapper script, use that wrapper instead of the raw `verilator` invocation above.

### 2) Build Rust firmware, if applicable

If the firmware must be compiled as part of co-simulation validation, build it with cargo:

```bash
cargo build --manifest-path "${FW_MANIFEST}" --release
```

If the firmware is already built by the firmware pipeline, this step may be skipped.

### 3) Run cocotb co-simulation via pytest

Run the cocotb test suite through pytest and direct all outputs into `firmware/validate/`:

```bash
pytest -q \
  --junitxml=firmware/validate/junit.xml \
  --log-cli-level=INFO \
  --cov \
  --cov-report=xml:firmware/validate/coverage_fw.xml \
  --cov-report=term \
  firmware/validate
```

If your cocotb tests live in a specific module or directory, set `COCOTB_TEST_MODULES` and/or point pytest at the appropriate test directory.

### 4) Collect RTL coverage outputs

If Verilator coverage is enabled in the build, collect the coverage artifacts expected by your simulator flow. Typical outputs may include:

- `firmware/validate/build/verilator/coverage.dat`
- merged coverage reports generated by `verilator_cov` or your local wrapper

Example post-processing commands:

```bash
verilator_coverage --annotate firmware/validate/coverage_rtl_annotated \
  firmware/validate/build/verilator/coverage.dat
```

Use the exact coverage command required by your environment if different.

## Expected Outputs

After a successful run, expect the following artifacts under `firmware/validate/`:

- `junit.xml` : pytest/cocotb test results
- `coverage_fw.xml` : firmware coverage report, if enabled
- RTL coverage data or merged coverage reports, if enabled
- simulator logs:
  - stdout/stderr from Verilator build
  - pytest/cocotb logs
- optional waveform traces, if your testbench enables them:
  - `*.vcd`, `*.fst`, or equivalent

Expected behavior in the logs:

- PWM enable path is exercised
- duty-cycle programming is reflected in `pwm_out`
- policy transitions occur at the requested temperature thresholds:
  - below 30 C: 0%
  - 30 C to 49 C: 25%
  - 50 C to 69 C: 55%
  - 70 C or above: 90%

## Validation Notes

This co-simulation is intended to verify:

- register writes from Rust firmware reach the RTL register layer correctly
- PWM enable, period, and duty-cycle programming behave as expected
- observable `pwm_out` matches the expected fan-control policy for all threshold regions
- coverage artifacts are generated when toggles are enabled

If the imported build collateral provides a dedicated runner script or make target, use that in preference to ad hoc commands.
